D7 Print L5 Evaluate Reference

Inkjet: How a Drop Is Fired

two ways to spit a picolitre of ink — boil a bubble behind it, or squeeze the chamber with a flexing crystal

A drop-on-demand nozzle ejects ink only when told to. Thermal (bubble-jet) heads flash-boil a microscopic bubble whose collapse kicks the drop out — cheap, but the violent collapse tends to shed trailing satellite droplets. Piezo heads flex a crystal wall to pressurise the chamber — gentler, cleaner drops, and they can fire inks the heater would cook.

Switch the actuator and watch the chamber: a growing-then-collapsing vapour bubble, or a wall flexing inward. Raise the firing frequency to pack more drops into the stream. Thermal mode sheds a small satellite behind each drop.
